title: Chapter 26: The Rehearsal
content: The motel room smelled like burnt coffee and ozone. Three laptops sat on the fake-wood dresser, their screens casting overlapping pools of blue-white light across the peeling wallpaper. Cables snaked between them like vines — HDMI, Ethernet, a tangle of USB adapters that Chloe had soldered together with a butane pen and steady hands. A portable fan whirred in the corner, doing nothing about the heat but making the air move, which was something.
Chloe pressed her palm flat against the center laptop. "Ready?"
Maya sat cross-legged on the bed across from her, a legal pad on her knee, pen capped between her fingers. "Go."

Chloe's fingers moved across the keyboard. On the left screen, a schematic of Aeterna's broadcast architecture bloomed into view — a branching tree of nodes, relay stations, fiber trunk lines, and the central broadcast hub at their Menlo Park campus. Red dots marked firewall checkpoints. Green lines showed the content delivery network that would carry Aeterna's IPO broadcast to every financial news outlet on the planet.

"Okay," Chloe said. "This is the path. Signal originates here —" she tapped a cluster of transmitters in the Midwest, legacy AM towers that Aeterna had quietly acquired through shell companies "— and rides the backbone to Menlo Park. From there it hits the broadcast chain: ingest, encode, multiplex, distribute. Every screen. Every phone. Every ticker in every trading floor on earth."

She highlighted a single node in yellow. "This is Kapoor's access point. He's the network architect. When he opens the firewall, it creates a gap in the content authentication layer. Our payload enters here." She typed. The center screen showed a progress bar filling — INJECTION SEQUENCE INITIATED. "Payload moves through the multiplexer, replaces the legitimate broadcast stream, and for ninety seconds, the world sees what Aeterna has done."
The right screen flickered. Then it displayed a frozen frame: a document. Internal Aeterna memo. Subject line: SUBJECT RETENTION PROTOCOL — SITE C. The camera in Maya's chest went cold.

"Full evidence package," Chloe said. "Financials. Medical records. The purchase orders for Site C construction materials. Names, dates, authorization signatures. Ninety seconds of proof that this company built a prison and filled it with women."
Maya uncapped her pen. Wrote: SIMULATION = SUCCESS. Underlined it twice. "How does this look in real life?"

Chloe leaned back. The office chair groaned. She rubbed her eyes with the heels of her hands — she'd been up since four, and it showed in the red rims, the gray pallor of her skin beneath the screen-glow.

"Shorter answer: it works. I've run this simulation fourteen times. Thirteen clean runs. One failure because I accidentally introduced a duped packet and the multiplexer rejected the payload as corrupted data."
"And the longer answer?"

"In real life, there are latency issues. Network congestion. Aeterna's IT team will be monitoring the broadcast chain like hawks on IPO day — it's the most critical technical event of their corporate life. If they detect the injection before we're live, they can cut the feed. Switch to a backup stream. Relegitimize the broadcast in under ten seconds." She held up her hand, fingers splayed. "Five things have to go right simultaneously. Kapoor opens the firewall at exactly the right moment. The payload transmits without packet loss. The multiplexer accepts the replacement stream. The CDN delivers it without buffering. And you —" she pointed at Maya "— are in position to make sure Vlad doesn't hit the kill switch."
"The kill switch," Maya repeated.

"Manual override. Physical button. Behind the bell, left panel, red. Marcus said three seconds to activate. If Vlad reaches it, the broadcast dies and all of this—" she gestured at the three laptops, the cables, the architecture of their conspiracy "— was for nothing."

Maya nodded. She looked at her legal pad. At the careful notes she'd taken over the past week: Kapoor's access window. The broadcast chain topology. The layout of the NYSE floor. Vlad's patterns. The kill switch. She'd drawn it all in neat block letters, no cursive, no shorthand. Every detail legible. Every detail survivable.
"Let's practice my part," she said.

The motel room was twelve feet by fourteen. Maya had measured it with a shoelace and a Sharpie, marking distances on the carpet. Four feet from the door to the first bed — that was the media entrance. Seven feet to the dresser — that was the AV corridor. Nine feet to the bathroom door — that was the podium. She'd taped an X on the carpet with blue painter's tape three feet from the bathroom. That was her position.

She stood at the door now. The forged badge hung from a lanyard around her neck — Chloe's best work. The photo was Maya's, but the name read K. MORRISON, and below it: BLOOMBERG LP — AV TECHNICIAN. The holographic strip shimmered when she tilted it. It would pass a visual inspection. It might not survive a database check.
Maya adjusted the lanyard. Straightened her shoulders. Walked forward.
"Name?" Chloe said from the bed, playing security.
"K. Morrison. Bloomberg AV. I'm here to check the audio feed on podium two."
"Badge."

Maya held it up, tilted it to catch the light, tucked it back. Kept walking. Seven feet. Nine feet. She stopped at the X on the carpet and turned to face the bathroom door — the podium. Left side, three feet away: the kill switch panel. She could see it in her mind. Behind the bell. Left panel. Red button.
"Time," she said.
Chloe checked her phone. "Forty-eight seconds."
"Forty-seven last time."
"You paused at the dresser. Adjusted your lanyard. That's a tell. If security's watching, that hesitation reads as nerves."

Maya pressed her thumb against her eyebrow. The lanyard hadn't felt right. That was the problem — it never felt right. She wasn't an AV technician. She wasn't K. Morrison. She was a woman who'd spent six years inside Aeterna's walls, watching Vlad eat people's lives, and now she was going to stand three feet from his kill switch on the most important day of his existence and make sure he couldn't use it.
"Again," she said.
She walked back to the door. This time she didn't adjust the lanyard. Didn't hesitate. Enter, badge, walk, position. Clean and quick.
"Forty-four seconds," Chloe said.
"Better."
"Good enough?"
Maya looked at the X on the carpet. The blue tape was already peeling at the corners. "Nothing's good enough. But it'll have to be."

Chloe didn't argue. They both knew the truth: forty-four seconds was acceptable. Thirty was the floor — anything less meant she couldn't reach position before Vlad noticed her. Sixty was the ceiling — anything more and security would question why an AV tech was lingering. Forty-four was the pocket. Tight but viable.

Maya walked back to the X. Stood on it. Closed her eyes. In the darkness behind her eyelids, she saw the NYSE floor. The brass. The marble. The bell. The left panel. The red button. Three seconds to activate. She had to be close enough to stop him — physically, if necessary — before his hand reached that button.
She opened her eyes. "What haven't we accounted for?"

Chloe was quiet for a long time. She turned back to her laptops, pulled up a waveform analysis, stared at it. The fan whirred. Coffee grew cold in paper cups. Outside, a truck rumbled past on the highway, its headlights sweeping across the curtain like a searchlight.
"The payload is incomplete," Chloe finally said.
Maya looked up from her legal pad. "What do you mean?"

"I mean it works. Ninety seconds of documents, financials, purchase orders, internal communications. It's enough to trigger an investigation. Enough to tank the stock. But it's missing the one thing Aeterna can't spin away."
"What?"

Chloe turned on the desk chair to face her. Her expression was uncharacteristically raw — no screens, no code, no architecture diagrams between them. Just two women in a hot motel room and the weight of what they were about to do.

"A voice," Chloe said. "A living victim speaking from captivity. Documents can be fabricated. Financials can be framed as creative accounting. But a woman saying 'my name is Lucy, I am being held against my will at this location, and these are the things they have done to me' — that's unspinnable. That overrides every denial they issue. That's the thing that makes jurors cry and senators demand hearings and shareholders run for the exits."
Maya's throat tightened. "We don't have a live feed from Site C."

"No. We don't. But—" Chloe turned back to the center laptop, pulled up a frequency spectrum. "Lucy said she has a shortwave antenna. She contacted us via Morse. Shortwave is analog. It doesn't need the internet. It doesn't need cell towers. It bounces off the ionosphere and lands wherever someone's listening."
"Can you receive shortwave and inject it into the broadcast?"

Chloe's fingers hovered over the keyboard. "Maybe. I have a software-defined radio receiver. It can tune to shortwave frequencies and digitize the audio in real time. If I'm receiving on the right frequency at the exact moment Lucy transmits, I can patch her signal into the broadcast chain alongside the documents. Her voice and the evidence, simultaneously."
"If," Maya said.

"If," Chloe agreed. "The timing has to be perfect. I need to know what frequency she's transmitting on. I need to be listening when she starts. And she needs to be transmitting when I patch her in — if I'm five seconds early or five seconds late, I'll capture dead air or miss her entirely. And shortwave reception in a motel room is—" she made a so-so gesture with her hand. "There's interference. Sferics. Power line noise. I'd need to move to a quieter location, something with a clear sky window. But none of that matters if Lucy isn't transmitting."
"Then we tell her when to transmit."
Chloe looked at her. "How?"
"The same way she contacted us. The transmitter network. Morse code."

Maya pulled her headphones from her bag — the same ones she'd used to intercept Lucy's first signal, the careful dots and dashes translating to a voice from a stone basement six women shared in the dark. She set them on the bed between them like a talisman.
"I'll record a message," Maya said. "We send it through the transmitter network. If she's still listening — if she's still at the antenna — she'll hear it."
"And if she's not?"
Maya's jaw tightened. "Then we proceed with what we have."

Maya sat on the edge of the bed with the portable audio recorder in her lap. Red light blinking. She'd rehearsed the message in her head three times, adjusting word by word until every syllable was load-bearing.
She pressed record.

"Lucy. IPO day. 9:30 AM Eastern. When you hear my voice on the broadcast, transmit everything on shortwave. Name, location, what they did to you. We'll patch you in live. You'll be heard by the entire world. Get to the antenna."

She stopped recording. Played it back. Twelve seconds. Clear, direct, no wasted breath. Chloe took the recorder, ran the audio through a signal processor, and beamed it into the transmitter network — a relay of low-power AM stations that Chloe had compromised one by one, each one a link in a chain that spanned from the Nevada desert to wherever Site C waited in the dark.

Whether the signal reached Lucy was unknown. Whether Lucy was still at the antenna was unknown. Whether she could transmit at the right moment was unknown. Three unknowns, each one capable of unraveling the plan's most powerful element.
They proceeded anyway. There was no other option.

Three hours later, Chloe sat hunched over the right laptop, monitoring Aeterna's network traffic through a series of backdoor sensors she'd embedded in their perimeter weeks ago. The screen scrolled with data — packet headers, authentication tokens, routing tables. Most of it was routine. Night-shift maintenance. Automated backups. The digital exhale of a corporation at rest.
Then Chloe sat up straight.
"Maya."
Something in her voice. Maya looked up from her legal pad.

Chloe pointed at the screen. "Sublevel B. They've tripled the security protocols in the last six hours. New access requirements. Biometric added to the keycard authentication. And here—" she highlighted a cluster of entries "— a second firewall around the broadcast system. Hardware-level. Not just software anymore."
"Vlad?"

"Has to be. He's hardening his defenses. The broadcast architecture I mapped two weeks ago? It's changed. The content authentication layer now has a redundant check — if the primary stream fails verification, a secondary system kicks in within two seconds. That means even if Kapoor opens the firewall and we inject the payload, the backup system can catch the discrepancy and swap to a clean stream."
"Can you still get through?"

Chloe's jaw worked. She typed rapidly, pulling up the new architecture, comparing it against their existing injection sequence. Red lines appeared where their plan no longer aligned with the new reality.

"With Kapoor's help? Yes. He designed both the primary and secondary systems. He can create a window where both are down. But—" she exhaled through her nose "—it's smaller now. The gap where both firewalls are open simultaneously. I had a hundred and twenty seconds. Now I might have ninety. Maybe less."
"Ninety seconds," Maya repeated. The same as the payload duration. No margin. No room for latency or congestion or a single dropped packet. Every millisecond would have to be perfect.
"If Kapoor's late by even a few seconds—or if the secondary system recovers faster than projected—" Chloe didn't finish the sentence. She didn't have to.
Maya looked at her legal pad. Crossed out 120. Wrote 90. The number looked thin on the page. Insufficient. She stared at it until the ink turned from blue to black.
"He knows something's coming," Chloe said quietly. "He doesn't know what. But he knows."
"Good," Maya said. "Let him worry. A worried man makes mistakes."
"A worried man also doubles his guards and adds firewalls," Chloe said. "Don't romanticize it."
Fair point. Maya didn't argue.

The bag was a black North Face duffel, borrowed from the motel's lost and found, washed twice, still faintly smelling of someone else's detergent — lavender and something chemical beneath it. Maya laid it open on the bed and began to pack.

One change of clothes. Black slacks, white button-down, the uniform of invisibility in corporate spaces. The forged badge went into the front pocket, lanyard coiled around it like a sleeping snake. Her headphones — the good ones, the ones that had heard Lucy's voice — went into the side compartment. Portable audio recorder. Sunglasses with the embedded camera, battery charged, sixteen gigabytes of empty storage waiting to capture whatever happened on the NYSE floor.
She stood over the bag. It didn't look like much. It looked like a weekend trip.
Chloe appeared beside her. She held something between two fingers — a thin rectangle the size of a credit card, matte black, no markings. She offered it like a communion wafer.
"What's this?" Maya took it. The card was cold, even in the overheated room. Thin as a bookmark. It weighed nothing.

"EMP pulse. One use. It'll fry any electronic device within three feet for about four seconds. I built it from a capacitor bank and a fast-rise-time inductor. Push the corner — here—" Chloe touched the upper-left edge of the card, and Maya felt a tiny raised dot beneath the surface "— and it discharges. No explosion, no spark. Just a magnetic pulse that turns silicon into scrap."
Maya turned the card over in her fingers. "What's this for?"

"The kill switch. If Vlad reaches for it and you can't stop him physically — fry it. The button is electronic. Capacitive sensor, not mechanical. Kill the circuitry, and he can press it all day. Nothing happens. He can't cut the feed with a button that doesn't work."
Four seconds. Three feet. The math was brutal — she'd need to be within arm's reach of the panel at the exact moment Vlad moved toward it. Close enough to touch it. Close enough for him to touch her.
Maya held the card against her palm. It was the most important object she'd ever held. A single-use miracle. One chance to turn a red button into dead plastic.
She slid it into her jacket pocket. The thin edge pressed against her hip, a line of cold through the fabric.
"Eleven days," she said.
Chloe nodded. Her eyes were red-rimmed and steady. "Then let's make them count."

Maya zipped the duffel. The sound was sharp and final in the quiet room — a metallic teeth-clack that sealed the transaction between planning and action. She lifted the bag by its handle. It was light. Too light for what it carried.
She set it by the door. The soldier's kit, waiting for war.
